coredata.dc:title: Smooth and dense graphene quantum dots-based lubricating coatings prepared by electrophoretic deposition
coredata.dc:creator: Qiang, Ruibin
Hou, Kaiming
Wang, Jinqing
Yang, Shengrong
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are very intriguing in various application fields of micro/nano-electrical-mechanical systems including biomedicine, photovoltaics, light-emitting diodes, and nanosensors devices due to their outstanding physical and chemical properties from quantum confinement and edge effects. Herein, GQDs were firstly synthesized, which can be well dispersed in water and the obtained dispersion shows high stability. Then, graphene quantum dots coatings (GQDCs) were deposited on silicon (Si) substrate via a simple electrophoretic deposition route by using GQDs aqueous dispersion as raw materials. As a result, the as-deposited GQDCs present very uniform, smooth and dense characters. The tribological measurements indicate that the optimum GQDCs sample shows effective reductions in friction coefficient (~85%) and wear volume (~97%) relative to those of the bare Si substrate. Based on these superior tribological performances, the deposited GQDCs are expected to be the outstanding solid lubricant coatings in Si-based devices with highly efficient lubrication and anti-wear properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots coatings
Electrophoretic deposition
Friction
Wear
Solid lubricant

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of GO nanosheets. (B), (C) TEM images of the as-prepared GQDs at different magnifications, the inset in (C) shows the size distribution of GQDs. (D) XPS spectrum of the as-prepared GQDs.

(A) Zeta potentials of GO and GQDs aqueous dispersions. (B) XPS spectrum of C1s of GQDs. (C) FTIR spectra of GO, GQDs, and GQDCs. (D) Raman spectrum of the GQDCs25, the inset shows the relative ratios of ID/IG for all GQDCs.

(A) Growth rate of GQDCs deposited at different voltages. (B) Optical images of different GQDCs. (C) Cross-sectional view of GQDCs30 with a high density. (D) SEM image of GQDCs35 with a dense deposition surface and cross-sectional view (inset) of GQDCs35.
(A) C1s and (B) O1s XPS spectra of GQDCs.
(A) The load–displacement curves of GQDCs. (B) Hardness, elastic modulus (Er), and elastic recovery of GQDCs.
(A) Friction coefficient–time curves of bare Si wafer and GQDCs. (B) Friction coefficient curves of Si, GOCs40, and GQDCs40. (C) The friction coefficient–deposition voltage curves of GQDCs and GOCs.
3D images of the wear tracks of Si (A), GQDCs20 (B), and GQDCs40 (C). The height profile measurements of the wear tracks (D) and the wear volumes (E) of Si and all GQDCs.
SEM micrographs of the wear tracks for GQDCs40 (A, B) and Si (C, D).
Schematic diagrams of preparing GQDCs by EPD and the corresponding friction test of GQDCs.
Ratio of C/O atoms, roughness, and thickness of the GQDCs.

head.ce:title: Smooth and dense graphene quantum dots-based lubricating coatings prepared by electrophoretic deposition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are very intriguing in various application fields of micro/nano-electrical-mechanical systems including biomedicine, photovoltaics, light-emitting diodes, and nanosensors devices due to their outstanding physical and chemical properties from quantum confinement and edge effects. Herein, GQDs were firstly synthesized, which can be well dispersed in water and the obtained dispersion shows high stability. Then, graphene quantum dots coatings (GQDCs) were deposited on silicon (Si) substrate via a simple electrophoretic deposition route by using GQDs aqueous dispersion as raw materials. As a result, the as-deposited GQDCs present very uniform, smooth and dense characters. The tribological measurements indicate that the optimum GQDCs sample shows effective reductions in friction coefficient (~85%) and wear volume (~97%) relative to those of the bare Si substrate. Based on these superior tribological performances, the deposited GQDCs are expected to be the outstanding solid lubricant coatings in Si-based devices with highly efficient lubrication and anti-wear properties.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, micro/nano-electrical-mechanical systems (M/NEMS) have obtained a flourishing development in various fields including microrobots, fine positioning deices, nanobiosensors, and so on. Namely, M/NEMS devices play an important role in many areas of our society. [1–4] However, the drawbacks of serious friction and wear in contact between moving mechanical parts weaken the practicability of M/NEMS devices and restrict their performance as well as operating life. [5–9] In order to solve the above drawbacks, various approaches have been developed to control or reduce the friction and wear. Among them, applying solid lubricant coatings between the contacting surfaces is probably one of the most effective strategies.

Solid lubricant coatings, one class of solid lubricants, have attracted a recent surge of interest due to their desirable tribological properties. [10–14] To date, many state-of-the-art solid lubricant coatings have been applied in solid lubricant fields including diamond-like carbon (DLC) coatings, [15–19] molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) coatings, [20–22] and graphene oxide (GO) coatings, [23] etc. Among them, GO coatings are considered to be the most promising and effective lubricant coatings due to their outstanding properties. [24–26] In spite of the large prospects of GO coatings, the widely used chemical vapor deposition (CVD) route is necessary way to fabricate GO coatings on large area. During the period of CVD, high preparation temperature and complicated transfer process increase the cost of production, which seriously impedes the development of GO coatings in practical applications. [13] Therefore, development of new cost-effective and fabrication-simple technique of GO coatings is urgently needed.

Recently, solution-based coating fabrication techniques of self-assembly and electrophoretic deposition (EPD) have been applied to obtain GO coatings, which are considered as a facile whilst economical choice. In particular, the EPD method is a powerful and productive technique that does not require expensive and complex equipment. [27–31] Previous work is successfully implemented to obtain GO coatings via EPD in GO suspension with Mg (NO3)2·6H2O as charged additive. [30] In fact, based on lots of negative charges on the surface of GO sheets, GO coatings are obtained by EPD in pure GO suspension without any charged additive. [23] However, the intrinsic drawbacks of GO sheets, such as easy accumulation and precipitation in water, lead to an inferior coating which is uneven and poor dense. [32] In this regard, challenges still remain despite extensive efforts to overcome the drawbacks.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one new type of zero dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, possess incredible properties and can be regarded as graphene sheets with transverse dimensions less than 100 nm. [33–34] More recently, some works have demonstrated that GQDs have superior biocompatibility, low toxicity, good water solubility, and unique optical/electrical performances, [35–37] and so on. Owing to these merits, GQDs have attracted extensive attention in many areas including biomedicine, photovoltaics, light-emitting diodes, and nanosensors devices. [38–41] However, there are few works involved on the application of GQDs in solid lubricant field. In fact, hydrophilic groups and small size effects make GQDs highly soluble in water, avoiding the aggregation of GO in water. [42–43] Therefore, it is conceivable to obtain dense GQDs coatings (GQDCs) through EPD route in a GQDs aqueous dispersion. In the present work, we attempt to utilize GQDs dispersion as the raw materials to develop a highly dense, uniform, and smooth solid lubricant coatings onto the surface of conductive silicon (Si) wafer via the EPD route. Furthermore, the structure and component of GQDCs as well as their tribological performances were investigated and discussed in detail.

Nature graphite powder (≤30 μm) originated from Qingdao Hensen Graphite Co., Ltd. N-type single crystal polished Si (1 0 0) wafers with resistivity of 0.01 were manufactured by Beijing Grinm Semiconductor Materials Co., Ltd. NaOH (≥96.0%), H2O2 (30%), HNO3 (65.0–68.0%) and H2SO4 (95.0–98.0%) were made in Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water (>18 MΩ·cm) was used in this work.

The preparation process of GQDs aqueous dispersion was described in detail in the Support Information (SI) section. Uniform and stable GQDs aqueous dispersion with the concentration of 2 mg/mL was subjected to several min sonication prior to deposition. Si wafers were first ultrasonically cleaned for 0.5 h in a mixture solution of 3 mL H2O2 and 7 mL H2SO4, and then rinsed with plenty of water. EPD system was adopted to fabricate GQDCs, as depicted in scheme 1 . Two Si wafers with a constant spacing distance of 5 mm were immersed in GQDs aqueous dispersion. Meanwhile, Si wafers, wires, and a DC power supply were connected together and different deposition voltages of 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, and 45 V were applied to energize for 1.5 h, the obtained coatings were denoted as GQDCs20, GQDCs25, GQDCs30, GQDCs35, GQDCs40, and GQDCs45, respectively. For comparison, series GO coatings (GOCs) were also prepared on Si wafers by EPD under the same deposition conditions with GQDCs series and the corresponding samples were denoted as GOCs20, GOCs25, GOCs30, GOCs35, GOCs40, and GOCs45. The detailed preparation can be found in SI section.

The zeta potentials of GO and GQDs aqueous dispersions were measured through a Laser dynamic scattering tester (Brookhaven, USA). The size and morphology characterizations of GO and GQDs were performed by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 TF20). A field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6701F, Japan) was used to explore the surface topography and section thickness of GOCs and GQDCs. Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM HR Evolution, France), FTIR spectrometer (Bruker IFS66v/S, Germany), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI-5702, Physical Electronics) were used to analyze the composition information of samples. A nanoindenter (TI-950, Hysitron, USA) was used to measure the mechanical properties of the GQDCs.

Tribological tests were conducted on a UMT-2MT tribometer (CETR) with a ball-on plate contact configuration. The GCr15 steel ball (φ = 3 mm, upper counterpart) reciprocally slides against a stationary plate with a distance of 5 mm, the sample to be tested was fixed on the plate. The normal load for all tests was 0.3 N, the frequency was 1 Hz, and the testing time was 1 h. The wear track of samples and roughness of GQDCs were investigated through SEM and a MicroXAM 3D noncontact surface mapping microscope profiler.

TEM image of GO (Fig. 1 A) shows that the lateral size of the corrugated GO sheets is several microns. In contrast, the TEM images (Fig. 1B and C) of GQDs indicate that the sample is consisted of a large amount of uniform GQDs with diameters of 1–3 nm, which is much smaller than the lateral dimension of GO. The statistic size distribution (Inset in Fig. 1C) indicates their average size is about 2 nm, and such a small size is beneficial to allow these GQDs’ accumulation into a dense coating during EPD process. The XPS survey spectrum of the as-prepared GQDs is shown in Fig. 1D, which reveals that the GQDs are only made up of carbon and oxygen elements. Meanwhile, the as-prepared GQDs have good dispersion in water and their aqueous dispersion can be preserved for several months without obvious precipitation. In this case, GQDs aqueous dispersion overcomes the dispersion bottleneck of GO nanosheets while avoids the aggregation or precipitation of GO in water throughout the EPD process, which is essential for obtaining high-quality coatings.

The zeta potentials of GO and GQDs aqueous dispersions under various mass concentrations were measured and shown in Fig. 2 A, which indicates that all the values are negative. It is obvious from Fig. 2A that all the zeta potentials (−3 to −12 mV) of GO aqueous dispersions are higher than those (−22 to −38 mV) of GQDs aqueous dispersions. In addition, the groups on the surface of the as-prepared GQDs were determined through XPS characterization. Fig. 2B shows the XPS spectrum of C1s of GQDs, three peaks (284.7, 286.3, and 288.8 eV) respectively correspond to CC/CC, CO, and CO groups, which means that the surfaces of GQDs have abundant hydrophilic groups. These results demonstrate that GQDs aqueous dispersions are negatively charged, providing the precondition for EPD route. Fig. 2C shows the FTIR spectra of the GO, GQDs, and GQDCs samples. For all samples, the broad peak at 3400 cm−1 corresponds to OH group; the peak at about 1432 cm−1 is assigned to COO group; moreover, the peaks at 1056 and 1136 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrational absorption band of CO; some peaks at 922, 815 and 627 cm−1 correspond to OH and CH, respectively. [44–45] In contrast to GQDs, the FTIR spectra of the GQDCs present the significant intensity decrease of surface chemical groups. This claims that the electrochemical reaction occurs at surfaces of the GQDs during the period of EPD. Compared the FTIR spectra of GO and GQDs, it is evident that there are the same groups appeared on their surfaces. Raman spectrum of GQDCs25 (Fig. 2D) shows two characteristic peaks at about 1353 and 1603 cm−1, which are assigned to the D band and G band, respectively, while the 2D peak is located at about 2700 cm−1. Fig. 2D reveals that the relative intensity ratio of ID/IG varies with the deposition voltage. The ID/IG value of the GQDCs25 is 1.03, which reaches the maximum at 40 V, meaning the disorder increase of GQDCs with the deposition voltage. [46]

As shown in Fig. 3 A, the growth rate and the deposition voltage are in approximately positive linear correlation with the increase of deposition voltage. Fig. 3B shows that the as-prepared GQDCs have different colors, which may be ascribed to the fact that different thickness of coatings absorbs different wavelength of light. [23] The cross-sectional SEM image of GQDCs30 (Fig. 3C) indicates the as-deposited GQDCs is well adhered on Si substrate with a high density and a very uniform thickness of about 175 nm. Similarly, surface morphology without apparent defects of GQDCs35 observed by SEM is shown in Fig. 2D. Smooth and highly dense structure is ascribed to the disordered and tight stacking of numerous GQDs. The inset in Fig. 3D shows the cross-sectional view of GQDCs35 with a high density and a uniform thickness of about 219 nm. For comparison, the surface morphology of GOCs35 and the cross-sectional view of GOCs40 are displayed in Figure S1A and S1B, respectively, which indicate that some obvious defects appear on the surface of GOCs35 and the thickness uniformity of GOCs40 is relative poor.

XPS survey spectra were measured and the corresponding results (Figure S2) show C1s peak at 284.7 eV and O1s peak at 532.6 eV for raw GQDs and the GQDCs. The C/O atomic ratio in GQDs is 1.65 while this ratio in GQDCs is obviously reduced. As summarized in Table 1 , the C/O atomic ratios in GQDCs range from 0.48 to 1.21. Some peaks of C1s (Fig. 4 A) respectively correspond to CC/CC (~284.7 eV), CO (~286.3 eV), and CO (~288.3 eV) groups, which may be ascribed to the retained hydrophilic groups on the surfaces of all GQDCs. The O1s peaks (532.6 eV) (Fig. 4B) of all GQDCs are nearly well-consistent with that of raw GQDs. Table 1 also shows the roughness of all the GQDCs determined by the 3D surface profiler and the roughness values of the GQDCs range from 4.28 to 5.36 nm, which varies with the deposition voltage and reaches the highest value at 25 V while the lowest value at 40 V. In addition, the thickness value (Table 1) of the GQDCs increases with the increase of deposition voltage.

The load–displacement curves of GQDCs were measured by the nanoindentation and the results are shown in Fig. 5 A. With improving the applied deposition voltage, the hardness and Young’s modulus of the GQDCs first increase and then slightly decrease, while the elastic recovery of GQDCs first decreases and then increases in the range from 38.72% to 54.74%, as clearly shown in Fig. 5B. Among all the GQDCs, the sample of GQDCs40 has the largest hardness and Young’s modulus values.

The tribological properties of the GQDCs in air environment were researched and the results are presented in Fig. 6 . As depicted in Fig. 6A, the bare Si substrate displays poor lubrication performance with a high friction coefficient of 0.43. Fortunately, the GQDCs display lower friction coefficients of 0.073, 0.113, 0.108, 0.079, 0.068, and 0.080, corresponding to the samples of GQDCs20, GQDCs25, GQDCs30, GQDCs35, GQDCs40, and GQDCs45, respectively, demonstrating that the GQDCs can significantly reduce the friction coefficient of Si substrate and can be considered as potential lubricating coatings for M/NEMS devices or other Si-based devices. Besides, all the friction curves of GQDCs are rather smooth, which is beneficial to steady running of M/NEMS devices. For comparison, the tribological properties of series GO coatings (GOCs) deposited at various voltages were also evaluated and the results are provided in Figure S3. Obviously, the GOCs display unstable friction curves and relative high friction coefficients of 0.143, 0.160, 0.156, 0.144, 0.187, and 0.368, corresponding to the samples of GOCs20, GOCs25, GOCs30, GOCs35, GOCs40, and GOCs45, respectively. Apparently, their friction coefficients are all higher than 0.14, which are also higher than those of the GQDCs. Fig. 6B compares the tribological behaviors of GQDCs40, GOCs40, and bare Si substrate. After a quite short running-in period, the friction coefficients of GQDCs40 and GOCs40 apparently decrease but the curve of GOCs40 is unstable, and the GQDCs40 exhibits the optimum tribological performances with the lowest friction coefficient among three samples. In addition, the change trends of friction coefficient with the deposition voltage for the GOCs and GQDCs are shown in Fig. 6C, indicating that friction coefficient of both series presents a similar change trend while GQDCs exhibit lower friction coefficient than GOCs. Meanwhile, the friction coefficient of all GQDCs varies with deposition voltage up to the highest value at 25 V whilst the lowest value at 40 V. Also, the friction coefficient of the GQDCs varies with the surface roughness (Table 1), hardness, Young’s modulus (Fig. 5B) and they are in approximately linear correlation. As illustrated in Scheme 1, the friction mechanism of GQDCs can be speculated as follows: during friction process, GQDs detached from GQDCs form small sliding graphene nanosheets on the friction interface, and such laminar slip effect makes the friction coefficient of GQDCs decrease significantly [47–50].

In addition, the anti-wear performance of solid lubricant coatings plays a vital role in M/NEMS application field on account of the service life, which will facilitate the M/NEMS devices to run smoothly and safely for a long term. Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 provide the wear volume, 3D and sectional morphology, as well as SEM image of the wear tracks of Si and GQDCs. As shown in Fig. 7A and 7D, bare Si has the wear track depth of about 0.63 μm, meaning the occurrence of severe wear. By contrast, it is obvious that GQDCs20 and GQDCs40 reveal mildly wear, and their wear tracks are relatively shallow with the depth values of about 0.08 and 0.11 μm (Fig. 7B, 7C and 7D). Among them, GQDCs20 was completely worn out because its thickness is only 56 nm while its wear depth is up to 110 nm. Luckily, GQDCs40 demonstrates excellent anti-wear performance that it has not been worn out because its wear depth is only 80 nm while its thickness is up to 277 nm. To further verify the excellent anti-wear performance, the wear tracks of the bare Si and GQDCs40 were also observed via SEM images. As exhibited in Fig. 8C and 8D, the wear track of the bare Si appears furrow scratches and a big wear width of about 104 μm, indicating that the bare Si is damaged seriously. As shown in Fig. 8A and 8B, the wear track of GQDCs40 is relatively smooth, and its smaller wear width of 53.2 μm is approximately 1/2 the width of Si, revealing mild wear.

As summarized in Fig. 7E, the bare Si wafer has a big wear volume of about 1.5 × 105 μm3 while all the GQDCs possess smaller wear values, the smallest wear volume of GQDCs40 is only 3.6 × 103 μm3, 1/42 the wear volume of Si wafer, indicating the GQDCs can significantly prolong the anti-wear performance of Si-based devices. These experimental results clearly demonstrate that the GQDCs40 possesses excellent tribological properties both in the lowest friction and the smallest wear volume. In a word, EPD technique is a green and economical route for fabricating GQDCs and the deposited GQDCs can find potential application as solid lubricant coatings in M/NEMS field.

In this work, EPD method was employed to prepare smooth and high dense GQDCs with the excellent tribological performances. Based on the facts that the GQDs own good water solubility and a mass of negative charges, the uniform GQDCs were achieved on conductive Si substrates in GQDs aqueous dispersions by EPD. The thickness and roughness values of the GQDCs can be tuned via altering the deposition voltages. Meantime, the GQDCs prepared under higher deposition voltages have higher density as well as higher hardness and Young’s modulus. The surface roughness of the GQDCs is approximately positively correlated with their friction coefficient, namely, the smaller the roughness is, the smaller of the friction coefficient will be. In short, the experiment results demonstrate that the GQDCs prepared by EPD route are able to reduce the friction coefficient and extend the wear life of Si-based devices remarkably. Among the deposited coatings, GQDCs40 shows the optimum tribological performances with the lowest friction and the best anti-wear performance, which can be employed as potential solid lubricant coatings in M/NEMS application field.

Ruibin Qiang: Writing - original draft, Software, Data curation. Kaiming Hou: ,Methodology, Validation, Funding acquisition. Jinqing Wang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Shengrong Yang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51575507, 51675514, and 51805518).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.145338.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vitro immunogenicity of silicon-based micro-and nanostructured surfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ainslie, S. Tao, K. Popat, T. Desai, In vitro immunogenicity of silicon-based micro-and nanostructured surfaces. ACS Nano 5 (2008) 1076–1084.

K. Naik, M. Hanay, W. Hiebert, X. Feng, M. Roukes, Towards single-molecule nanomechanical mass spectrometry. Nat. Nanotechnol. 4 (2009) 445–450.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Friction and wear properties of different types of graphene nanosheets as effective solid lubricants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Peng, Z. Wang, K. Zou, Friction and wear properties of different types of graphene nanosheets as effective solid lubricants. Langmuir 31 (2015) 7782−7791.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel running-in method for improving life-time of bulk-fabricated silicon MEMS devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Shen, Y. Meng, A novel running-in method for improving life-time of bulk-fabricated silicon MEMS devices. Tribol. Lett. 47 (2012) 273−282.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vapor lubricant transport in MEMS devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Gellman, Vapor lubricant transport in MEMS devices. Tribol. Lett. 17 (2004) 455−461.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Donnet, A. Erdemir, Solid lubricant coatings: recent developments and future trends. Tribol. Lett. 17 (2004) 389−397.
S. Stankovich, D. Dikin, G. Dommett, K. Kohlhaas, E. Zimney, E. Stach, Graphene-based composite materials. Nature 442 (2006) 282−286.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impermeable atomic membranes from graphene sheets

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembled graphene film as low friction solid lubricant in macroscale contact

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wu, X. Li, C. Zhang, X. Chen, S. Lin, H. Sun, C. Lin, H. Zhu, J. Luo, Self-assembled graphene film as low friction solid lubricant in macroscale contact. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 21554−21562.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toward zero micro/macro-scale wear using periodic nano-layered coatings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Oleksiy, A. Penkov, M. Devizenko, N. Evgeniy, V. Kondratenko, D. Kim, Toward zero micro/macro-scale wear using periodic nano-layered coatings. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 7 (2015) 18136−18144.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Understanding run-in behavior of diamond-like carbon friction and preventing diamond-like carbon wear in humid air

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Marino, E. Hsiao, Y. Chen, O. Eryilmaz, A. Erdemir, S. Kim, Understanding run-in behavior of diamond-like carbon friction and preventing diamond-like carbon wear in humid air. Langmuir 27 (2011) 12702–12708.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Shear-induced structural changes and origin of ultralow friction of hydrogenated diamond-like carbon (DLC) in dry environment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Manimunda, A. Al-Azizi, S. Kim, R. Chromik, Shear-induced structural changes and origin of ultralow friction of hydrogenated diamond-like carbon (DLC) in dry environment. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 16704−16714.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, J. Xu, Y. Ootani, S. Bai, Y. Higuchi, N. Ozawa, K. Adachi, J. Martin, M. Kubo, Tight-binding quantum chemical molecular dynamics study on the friction and wear processes of diamond-like carbon coatings: effect of tensile stress. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 34396−34404.
